segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: It's a simple but useful tool that is fun to use.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: Chrome not working with the required version of JavaScript.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: Matching code snippets to O notation is a very useful skill.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: No undo/redo.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: The exploits were fun and interesting.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: None, it's great.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: It's very well put together, everything is laid out simply and is very easy to understand what is going on.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: None!

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: Gives hints as to how to parse text and is easy to use.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: Some text on the exact purpose of the exercise would help in a couple of sections.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: It's hands-on, free form coding answers - no limit on individual creativity in finding solutions

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: Inability to view previous answers

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: Might be improved by understanding real world frequency of bugs and evaluating responses against that - so a simple fix which passes the test would score lower than one that passed the test but included logic that covered likely issues in a production environment.  I guess context would be the word

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: The feedback on answers

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: The feeling of time pressure

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: Feels like you are interrogating a DB

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: If you do not get the bonus questions on level 1 - you cannot progress to the next level - or at least there is no obvious way to do so

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: It's quick to make progress

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: Hints are a little too easy - the other hints still needed some application - I do see that you lose points so maybe thats OK

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: I actually forgot the task.  Even though its visually represented, the initial challenge could be presented on the game page

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: Great tool into the mysterious world of regular expressions - these cannot be learnt from reading - you do need an interactive tool

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: Remembering what the task is - you get this wording between levels and its easy to forget

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: the level system delivered the learning outcomes in a repetitive way that was not tiresome, but engaging with the 3 different tasks per level. repeatedly writing test cases for instance solidified the syntax in muscle memory a bit more.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: the code editor was challenging to spot syntax issues being in plain text with no colours or lint feedback.

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: integrating the source code directly into the tooling ui would remind the learner to read through it some more as the link to the source code is easily missed at the start. Also reading the source code to 'cheat' is valuable learning in itself. making it more accessible could prevent a tired learner from asking what the answer is from AI and avoiding the learning outcomes.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: the different ways of interacting was good, instead of having all levels as a 'choose abcd' format. ending on the comparison was very good as it cemented the learning well through code analysis.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: because I didn't really know about big-o notation going into the tool I did feel a bit lost and ended up guessing.

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: sound effects on scoring mechanics could give more reward and excitement to the learner.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: learning at the point of view from an attacker is very valuable. the ui was fun to use and contained a lot of data which was laid out in a very usable way.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: the hints could be revealed from most useful (the last one) to least useful (first one). this would enable the most valuable hint to be revealed first and cause the least amount of point loss.

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: an option where no points would be given but the solution to the problem would be revealed could be beneficial to allow the learner to still progress if they were stuck on answer and would also allow the user to learn from the solution that they were stuck on.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: the code input mimics a traditional coding environment with linting/colours etc.

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: the prompt is easily missed if not paying attention and the dialog is dismissed immediately

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: the relevant options for a css property could be displayed somewhere to help guide the user e.g. listing what are the accepted values for the align-items property are somewhere. perhaps as a hint even.

segments.label: Expert — Most Positive
segments.text: showing the possible regex components underneath the input

segments.label: Expert — Most Negative
segments.text: the regex components were not actively explained as the user selected them. the question the user had to answer was not present during the interaction stage.

segments.label: Expert — Other Comments
segments.text: using something like tool tips instead of dialogs could help keep the user more informed, reduce the risk of missing information and allow the user to progress at their own pace. for example showing the success ui immediately instead of allowing the user to digest the success state of the data vs the regex.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Benefit
segments.text: This can replace a large chunk of passive theoretical learning in one of the lectures during the semester.  It provides a novel  in class way to teach a difficult programming concept.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Drawback
segments.text: I think there were some mobile responsive issues with the page.  It is probably best done on a laptop screen.  I would have to ensure the students bring a laptop to class,  or maybe engage with the developer to improve the implementation for mobile.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Benefit
segments.text: As the framework is provided, the student can focus on the specific task at hand.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Drawback
segments.text: When more complex (multi-stage) tasks are the objective, this would need to provide less support so that the student has to construct the flow of logic without hints.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Benefit
segments.text: Clear and accessible explanation of the SQL injection concept through a practical example combined with hands-on experience, enabling students to understand both the theory and its real-world implications.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Benefit
segments.text: Provides engaging, relevant material while relieving the ML of the effort of constructing it

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Drawback
segments.text: It needs a few small refinements to be truly effective: the initial exercise should be a video example. And the descriptive prompt should stay on screen as you work through each  regular-expression exercises. I also note that the regular expression matches more in the sample code than it means to: the pre dot and post dot part of a float (for integers), keywords for identifiers etc. That is quite confusing.

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Benefit
segments.text: Gives students practical opportunities to come to understand one of the fundamental aspects of algorithms

segments.label: Coordinator — Most Significant Drawback
segments.text: I thought the timeout was a little too fast; the plotting was confusing -- see my botched attempts! :-)  Also, for, say, a linear-time algorithm would I have got correct marks if I plotted f(n)=20n?  I didn't even get that far but the examples seemed to assume a constant of C=1.  That's being extremely nit-picky :-).
